notes.anchor: what you did with Curio in the matter of the supplication
notes.note: A supplicatio was a public thanksgiving voted by the Senate to a victorious commander, a necessary preliminary step toward a triumph; Caelius had lobbied Curio as tribune to support the motion for Cicero's supplicatio, and the man who was hostile (a rival candidate, possibly M. Marcellus) had assented.

notes.anchor: the things which you hope can be moderated by my Tullia's good sense
notes.note: Dolabella, newly betrothed to Tullia, had a reputation for extravagance and erratic behaviour; Caelius apparently hoped Tullia's steadiness would steady him, and Cicero responds with gentle irony that he had said as much to Appius.

notes.anchor: I favour Curio; I want Caesar to come off honourably; for Pompey I am willing to die
notes.note: Cicero's famous three-part declaration of his political loyalties in the gathering crisis of 50 BC, written just months before Caesar crossed the Rubicon; the uneasy triple loyalty captures his effort to remain useful to all parties while Rome moved toward civil war.

notes.anchor: I have set the quaestor, Coelius, in charge of it
notes.note: Roman governors departing a province were obliged to leave a senior officer in command; the quaestor was the normal choice when no legate of higher standing was available. Cicero's self-deprecating framing ('A boy?') pre-empts exactly the criticism he anticipated.

notes.anchor: the two most powerful men in the state, who have embraced all the Cassii and Antonii
notes.note: Cicero alludes to Pompey and Caesar, each of whom had cultivated entire noble families as clients and lieutenants — the Cassii and the Antonii are cited as representative clans absorbed into one or the other's political orbit.

The reference is to some public affair involving a Matrinius that had made Caelius famous even in the provinces beyond the Taurus; the detail is otherwise lost, but it was evidently a forensic or political coup by Caelius as aedile.
